title: Mission Creek Stormwater Park - CMG Landscape Architecture
content: # Mission Creek Stormwater Park. Mission Creek Stormwater Park is a leading example of green infrastructure, with a focus on urban stormwater treatment in bioswales and bioretention basins, shoreline stabilization, and ecology. The multi-use promenade serves as a dedicated pedestrian and bike entrance to the new Mission Bay neighborhood in San Francisco. Set along Mission Creek, the waterfront experience is met with unmatched views of San Francisco Bay, the San Francisco Giants’ ballpark, and Twin Peaks. Stormwater treatment was the key design factor and function for the park – the San Francisco Public Utilities Commission requires the park to treat offsite stormwater runoff from 13.8 acres of adjacent open space and streets. ### Case Study of Mission Creek Stormwater Park Published in LAF’s Landscape Performance Series. Mission Creek Stormwater Park is recognized by the American Society of Civil Engineers San Francisco Section for its outstanding sustainable and environmental engineering.

title: Rain Gardens & Bioswales - The Watershed Project
content: ## Rain Gardens & Bioswales. Green infrastructure on the Richmond Greenway. Rain Gardens and Bioswales are examples of ***green infrastructure*** and use natural functions to capture and filter stormwater. ***What is a rain garden?***. A rain garden is a garden of native plants that is watered by stormwater. A rain garden accomplishes stormwater filtration by collecting water on low gradients, slowing it, and allowing the water to infiltrate into a porous surface, instead of flowing across asphalt or concrete. Rain gardens and bioswales capture the stormwater and slow it down. Through this process, the rain gardens and bioswales help clean the water before it enters the San Francisco Bay. The native plants in the rain gardens and bioswales also create habitat for birds and butterflies. With community input and volunteer assistance, The Watershed Project constructs bioswales and rain gardens across Richmond and San Pablo, including a network of 14 bioswales and rain gardens along the Richmond Greenway.
